title: PRISM
text:
PRISM is a comprehensive framework designed to advance auditory scene analysis (Ecological Momentary Assessment) for hearing devices. This repository contains the code, machine learning models, and tools developed by and for the “Australian Future Hearing Initiative”.
PRISM might stand for:
Modern hearing aids often struggle to adapt to rapidly changing sound environments—like moving from a quiet room to a busy street or a noisy restaurant. PRISM and related technologies help bridge the gap between raw audio processing and intelligent environmental awareness, leading to a more seamless and personalised hearing experience for users.

title: PRISM
text:
Capabilities

A web portal for researchers to run experiments and view results.
An Android mobile and Watch app for participant data collection.
A backend server for data storage and processing.
A set of machine learning models for auditory scene analysis.

title: PRISM
text:
Citation
If you use these capabilities from the code, model, or datasets in your research, please cite:
@misc{zhong2026datasetmodelauditoryscene,
      title={A dataset and model for auditory scene recognition for hearing devices: AHEAD-DS and OpenYAMNet}, 
      author={Henry Zhong and Jörg M. Buchholz and Julian Maclaren and Simon Carlile and Richard Lyon},
      year={2026},
      eprint={2508.10360},

}

title: Developer Guide
text: The PRISM Ecosystem consists of a backend server built with FastAPI and a web frontend built with Flutter. Both rely on Firebase for authentication and database storage. This guide provides instructions on how to set up the PRISM system locally for development or deployment.

title: Developer Guide
text:
Firebase
Get a paid project on Firebase and make a new project.

title: Developer Guide
text:
Unified Setup via Docker
The easiest way to install and run both the PRISM Backend and Frontend is via the prism-setup repository, which uses Docker Compose.

1. Prerequisites
Ensure the following are installed and running: - Docker Desktop - must be installed and running! - Git for Windows, or for Mac and Linux git is pre-installed. - A Firebase project with paid usage enabled. - A Mailgun account or a Gmail account for sending emails.


2. First-time Setup

1. Clone the repository:
Clone this repository and move into it.
git clone https://github.com/Australian-Future-Hearing-Initiative/prism-setup.git
cd prism-setup


2. Configure your keys and secrets:
Create a .env file in the prism-setup directory with the following keys. You can also run the install script to create a template .env file for you.

1. JWT Secret
Generate a unique secret for your session tokens using openssl rand -hex 32 or from https://jwtsecretkeygenerator.com/ the set the value:
JWT_SECRET=xxxx


2. Email Settings
Option A: Mailgun

Sign up for a free account at https://www.mailgun.com/
Follow the instructions to add a domain and verify your email address.
Copy the API key and domain name into your .env file.

MAILGUN_API_KEY=xxxx
MAILGUN_AUTHORIZED_DOMAIN=xxxx.mailgun.org
Option 2. Via Gmail’s SMTP server
To allow PRISM to send emails via Gmail, you need to enable 2-Step Verification and generate an App Password. * Gmail App Password (MAIL_PASSWORD): Google Account → Security & Sign-in → turn 2‑Step Verification ON * Then navigate to your App passwords → create a new app password, give it a name (PRISM) copy the 16‑char code (no spaces) into MAIL_PASSWORD.
Add the following to your .env file:
MAIL_USERNAME=xxxxx@gmail.com
MAIL_FROM=xxxxxx@gmail.com            # must match or be a verified alias
MAIL_PASSWORD=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx      # 16‑char Google App Password, NOT EMAIL PASSWORD, see below
MAIL_SERVER=smtp.gmail.com
MAIL_PORT=587
MAIL_STARTTLS=True                  # or: MAIL_PORT=465 + MAIL_SSL_TLS=True
MAIL_SSL_TLS=False
MAIL_FROM_NAME=PRISM
USE_CREDENTIALS=True
VALIDATE_CERTS=True


3. Firebase Web API Key

You must have access to a paid Firebase project with an “Editor” or “Owner” role to be able to get a private key. Replace PrismData and prism (web) with the corresponding names of your Firebase project and web app below.

From the Firebase Console, find it in PrismData → Settings → General
Scroll down to Your apps → Web apps → prism (web) → Config (radio button) > apiKey, then add:
FIREBASE_API_KEY=xxxxx


4. Firebase Service Account Key file
From the Firebase Console: PrismData → Settings → Service accounts → Firebase Admin SDK → Generate new private key.
Rename the file to serviceAccountKey.json and place it in the prism-setup directory, then add:
FIREBASE_KEY_PATH=./serviceAccountKey.json




3. Run the installer script:
bash install.sh
This will create the Dockerfiles for the backend and frontend, clone the repos and check for the presence of your keys. If it fails, simply fix the errors as suggested and run the script again.


4. Launch PRISM
Now to launch the frontend and backend services on your local machine run:
docker compose up --build
Wait until setup completes then interact with the app via:

Frontend Dashboard: http://localhost:3000
Backend API: http://localhost:8000

To stop the frontend and backend services run:
docker compose down


5. Deploy to production
TODO

title: Developer Guide
text:
Manual Backend Setup
If you wish to run the FastAPI backend independently without Docker, follow the instructions as detailed in the prism-flutter-backend repository.

1. Set up a virtual environment

Option 1: Using uv (recommended for speed)

Make sure uv is installed:

uv --version
If not installed:
brew install uv             # macOS (Homebrew)
pipx install uv             # cross-platform alternative

Create a virtual environment

uv venv

Activate

source .venv/bin/activate


Option 2: Using venv

Delete the current virtual environment (If you created a virtual environment in another project)

rm -rf venv
or
rm -rf .venv

Create and activate the virtual environment

python -m venv venv
or
uv venv

Activate the virtual environment

source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

Q: Why use a virtual environment? A: It ensures isolated package versions, so dependencies from one project don’t affect another.





2. Add dependencies
Use
uv sync # This will install all dependencies that you need for the project
Tips: How to add new dependencies:
uv add your_dependency



3. Firebase & Environment variables Setup

FIREBASE_KEY_PATH
If you don’t have the access to the Firebase, please contact your firebase admin. For firebase Admin: They need to have “Editor” or “Owner” to be able to get the private key.

In Firebase Console: PrismData → Project settings → Service accounts → Firebase Admin SDK → Generate new private key.
Save the downloaded file as serviceAccountKey.json in this backend repo root.
Add .env by using

  touch .env
Open your .env:
nano .env
and add:
FIREBASE_KEY_PATH=./serviceAccountKey.json


FIREBASE_API_KEY
Find it in Project settings → General → prism (web) -> apiKey, then add:
FIREBASE_API_KEY=YOUR_WEB_API_KEY


JWT_SECRET
Generate a long random string by going to this website: https://jwtsecretkeygenerator.com/
Once you have got the JWT then copy it to here:
JWT_SECRET=YOUR_RANDOM_SECRET


Example .env
FIREBASE_KEY_PATH=./serviceAccountKey.json
FIREBASE_API_KEY=AIza...yourkey
JWT_SECRET=paste_a_long_random_string_here


Email (SMTP) Setup
Add these to your .env to enable outbound email:
Option 1. Via Mailgun: Get your API key from Mailgun API Key page, and setup Mailgun with a domain you can send from.
MAILGUN_API_KEY=your_mailgun_api_key
MAILGUN_AUTHORIZED_DOMAIN=yourdomain.com
Option 2. Via Gmail’s SMTP server
MAIL_USERNAME=your@gmail.com
MAIL_FROM=your@gmail.com            # must match or be a verified alias
MAIL_PASSWORD=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx      # 16‑char Google App Password
MAIL_SERVER=smtp.gmail.com
MAIL_PORT=587
MAIL_STARTTLS=True                  # or: MAIL_PORT=465 + MAIL_SSL_TLS=True
MAIL_SSL_TLS=False
MAIL_FROM_NAME=PRISM
USE_CREDENTIALS=True
VALIDATE_CERTS=True
MAIL_PASSWORD=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx: Google.com -> Google Account → Security → turn 2‑Step Verification ON → App passwords → generate one, copy the 16‑char code (no spaces) into MAIL_PASSWORD.
Troubleshooting (535 BadCredentials): use the app password (not your login), choose exactly one TLS mode (587+STARTTLS or 465+SSL/TLS), restart after .env changes, and ensure MAIL_FROM is allowed.



3. Run the development server
Navigate to the directory where main.py is located, then run:
uv run -- uvicorn app.main:app --reload
or
uvicorn app.main:app --reload


4. Deactivate the virtual environment when you’re done
deactivate
This returns your shell to the global Python environment.



5. View API documentation
Once the server is running, you can explore the API docs at:

Swagger UI: http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs

ReDoc: http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc

These documents list all available endpoints and describe how to interact with them.



6. Debug
You should go to the root and run
uv run -- python -m debugpy --listen 127.0.0.1:5678 --wait-for-client \
  -m uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
Then you can run the debugger.


7. Test
uv run pytest


📁 Project Structure
app/
├── main.py                 # FastAPI application entry point
├── api/
│   └── routes/             # Route definitions (e.g., /experiments, /participants)
├── domain/                 # Pydantic models and schemas
├── services/               # Core business logic (e.g., experiment handling, exports)
├── infrastructure/         # Integrations with Firebase, Firestore, and email
└── utils/                  # Helper functions (e.g., CSV export utilities)

requirements.txt            # Python package dependencies
.env                        # Environment variable configuration (not committed)
README.md                   # Project overview and instructions
This structure follows a modular design that separates concerns across routing, logic, data modeling, and infrastructure. It ensures maintainability and scalability of the backend.



📬 Contact
Alan Wang

title: Developer Guide
text:
Manual Frontend Setup
If you wish to run the front end Flutter web application without docker, follow the instructions as detailed in the prism-flutter-web-frontend repository.
The PRISM web frontend serves as the interface for data collection and study management for Researchers conducting “experiments” within the PRISM ecosystem as part of the Australian Future Hearing Initiative.


For Researchers
Researchers use this application to design experiments, manage study cohorts, and deploy tasks to participants. It is the control center for your data collection needs.
How to use: 1. Login: Access the researcher dashboard securely. 2. Manage Projects: Create new projects to house specific studies or experiments. 3. Configure Experiments: * Set up various experiment types (e.g., Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) surveys, sensor-thresholds). * Define parameters and logical flows for the experiments. 4. Manage Participants: * Add new participants to the system. * Assign participants to specific projects/cohorts. * Monitor participant progress through assigned tasks. * Email participants. * Download audiograms
See the complete userguide: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1_FZVBiN1QqhP50A2izS7KlW_Y021koJM45pSYcHEkMw/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.qbhdu5cvo4bq



Developer Instructions

1. Launch the Backend Server
This frontend relies on the prism-flutter-backend. See the repo for launch instructions.


2. Configure Environment
Create a .env file in the root directory and add the URL of the backedn server, e.g.:
## Base URL for API calls when running locally
API_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8000


3. Install FVM (Flutter Version Management)
We use FVM to ensure consistent Flutter versions.

Option 1 Dart Pub: dart pub global activate fvm
Option 2 Homebrew: brew tap leoafarias/fvm && brew install fvm
Option 3 Install Script: curl -fsSL https://fvm.app/install.sh | bash



4. Run the App
Execute the following commands from the root of this repository:
fvm flutter channel stable
fvm flutter upgrade
fvm flutter pub get
## fvm dart run build_runner watch -d    # Optional: watch freezed/json files for changes
fvm flutter run -d chrome             # Run the app in Chrome


🧪 Testing & Architecture

Unit & Widget Tests: flutter test
Specific Test: flutter test <path/to/test.dart>
Integration Tests:
flutter drive --driver=test_driver/integration_test.dart --target=integration_test/participant_flow_test.dart -d chrome
Note: Web integration tests inject bytes directly as WebDriver screens cannot handle drag-and-drop.
Coverage: flutter test --coverage

Key Directory Structure: * lib/ui: Contains all screens and widgets, separated by feature (e.g., experiment, participant, auth). * lib/domain: Business logic and models. * lib/data: Data repositories and API services.


TODO: Deployment Instructions

title: Background
text:
Hearing loss affects over 1.5 billion people worldwide and is one of the leading contributors to reduced quality of life. Modern hearing aids are increasingly sophisticated, yet they still struggle to adapt to rapidly changing sound environments like moving from a quiet office to a busy street, a noisy restaurant, or a reverberant lecture hall.
Traditional hearing-aid fitting is performed in a clinical setting with a controlled acoustic environment, but real-world listening situations are far more varied and unpredictable. There is a well-documented gap between how a hearing device performs in the clinic and how it performs in the wild. Bridging this gap requires understanding when, where, and why users experience difficulty — and that understanding can only come from data collected in the user’s everyday life.

title: Background
text:
Hearing loss affects over 1.5 billion people worldwide and is one of the leading contributors to reduced quality of life. Modern hearing aids are increasingly sophisticated, yet they still struggle to adapt to rapidly changing sound environments like moving from a quiet office to a busy street, a noisy restaurant, or a reverberant lecture hall.
Traditional hearing-aid fitting is performed in a clinical setting with a controlled acoustic environment, but real-world listening situations are far more varied and unpredictable. There is a well-documented gap between how a hearing device performs in the clinic and how it performs in the wild. Bridging this gap requires understanding when, where, and why users experience difficulty — and that understanding can only come from data collected in the user’s everyday life.

title: Background
text:
Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA)
PRISM is built around the methodology of Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), a research technique that captures data from participants in their natural environments, in real time, rather than relying on retrospective recall in a lab.
In a PRISM experiment, participants carry a smartphone (and optionally a smartwatch) that:

Captures a brief audio snapshot, a short recording of the acoustic environment.
Prompts a self-report, the participant rates their current listening experience via simple feedback buttons (e.g., Good, Okay, Bad).
Collects wearable sensor data, physiological and inertial signals that provide additional context about the listener’s state and activity.

This approach provides ecologically valid, time-stamped, multi-modal data that links subjective hearing difficulty to objective environmental and physiological measurements.

title: Background
text:
Multi-Modal Sensor Data
A key design principle of PRISM is that hearing difficulty cannot be understood from audio alone. The platform collects data from multiple sensor modalities across phone and watch devices:








Sensor
Device
Purpose




Microphone
Phone, Watch
Captures environmental audio for acoustic scene classification


Heart Rate
Watch
Physiological indicator of stress or cognitive effort


Body Temperature
Watch
Additional physiological context


Accelerometer
Phone, Watch
Activity recognition (walking, sitting, commuting)


Gyroscope
Phone, Watch
Complements accelerometer for motion and posture estimation



Each sensor is independently configurable per experiment where researchers can set sampling rates, enable or disable raw data upload, and define thresholds that control when data collection or model inference is triggered.

title: Background
text:
On-Device Machine Learning
PRISM integrates machine learning models that run alongside the data collection pipeline. These models serve two primary roles defined in the platform:

YAMNet+ - Acoustic Scene Classification
YAMNet+ is an environment analysis model that classifies the acoustic scene surrounding the participant (e.g., open space, restaurant, traffic, quiet room). It takes normalised float32 audio as input and produces scene labels, enriching each data sample with an automatic environmental context tag.
This builds on the YAMNet architecture and is described in:

Zhong, H., Buchholz, J. M., Maclaren, J., Carlile, S., & Lyon, R. (2026). A dataset and model for auditory scene recognition for hearing devices: AHEAD-DS and OpenYAMNet. arXiv:2508.10360



WONU - Communication Breakdown Detection
The Words of Not Understanding (WONU) model detects linguistic cues in speech that indicate a communication breakdown. Phrases such as “pardon?”, “sorry, what?”, or “can you repeat that?” are examples of such cues. These cues are a direct, in-the-moment signal that a hearing-aid user is struggling to follow conversation, making WONU a complementary signal to the participant’s self-report.

title: Background
text:
Rule-Based Triggers
Researchers can configure sensor-threshold triggers that automate data collection and model activation. A trigger is defined by:

A sensor source (e.g., phone microphone, heart rate monitor).
A metric and threshold (e.g., sound level > 70 dB, heart rate > 100 BPM, temperature > 37 °C).
An action - what happens when the threshold is crossed:

Activate an ML model - wake YAMNet+ or WONU to classify the current moment.
Send a notification - prompt the participant to provide a self-report.
Launch a form - open an in-app or Google Form survey for richer contextual data.


This trigger system allows PRISM experiments to be event-driven rather than purely scheduled, capturing data precisely when listening conditions are most challenging or interesting.

title: Background
text:
Data Pipeline
Data flows from participant devices through a cloud pipeline:

On-device capture - the mobile app records audio, sensor streams, and self-reports, tagging each sample with experiment, participant, and device identifiers.
Cloud storage - files are uploaded to Firebase Storage using a structured path convention.
Automatic indexing - a Cloud Function mirrors each upload into Firestore, creating a searchable, filterable catalogue of responses with metadata including ML classification results, timestamps, and file sizes.
Researcher dashboard - the PRISM web frontend lets researchers browse, filter, play back, and download participant responses, grouped by experiment and data type (audio recordings, heart rate, accelerometer, gyroscope, body temperature).

title: Background
text:
Why PRISM?
PRISM addresses a key bottleneck in hearing science: the lack of large-scale, ecologically valid, multi-modal datasets linking real-world acoustic environments to user-reported hearing difficulty. By providing an end-to-end platform, from experiment design and participant recruitment through data collection and ML-assisted analysis, PRISM enables researchers to:

Study hearing difficulty where it actually happens, not just in the lab.
Combine subjective self-reports with objective sensor and ML-derived features.
Build and validate models that can eventually drive real-time, adaptive hearing-aid algorithms.
Rapidly iterate on experiment designs through a configurable web interface.

The long-term vision, as part of the Australian Future Hearing Initiative, is a future where hearing devices automatically recognise difficult listening situations and adjust their processing in real time — giving users seamless hearing across every environment they encounter.

title: Background
text:
Models
Tools and scripts for AuditoryHuM auditory scene clustering, AHEAD-DS data preparation and OpenYAMNet(YAMNet+) training/inference.

About
This repo supports the AuditoryHuM/AHEAD-DS/OpenYAMNet papers by providing: - clustering pipline for AuditoryHuM - dataset build scripts for AHEAD-DS and AHEAD-DS-unmixed - training pipeline for OpenYAMNet/YAMNet+ on those datasets - legacy YAMNet conversion utilities


Project Map

prism-ml-auditoryhum/: auditory scene label discovery and clustering using MLLM label generation and zero-shot learning.
prism-ml-dataset-ahead-ds/: build AHEAD-DS and AHEAD-DS-unmixed from raw sources (large downloads). See that README for full instructions and warnings about size/time.
prism-ml-yamnetp-tune/: train and evaluate OpenYAMNet/YAMNet+ using AHEAD-DS, plus inference and TFLite conversion.
prism-ml-yamnet-legacy/: convert the original YAMNet weights to TF SavedModel format compatible with TF 2.16+.



Quick Start
Follow the relevant README: - Auditory scene clustering: prism-ml-auditoryhum/README.md - Dataset build: prism-ml-dataset-ahead-ds/README.md - Model training/inference: prism-ml-yamnetp-tune/README.md - Legacy YAMNet conversion: prism-ml-yamnet-legacy/README.md


Paper, Data and Models

AuditoryHuM paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19409
AuditoryHuM supplementary data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/hzhongresearch/auditoryhum_supplementary
AuditoryHuM demo website with alignment samples: https://huggingface.co/spaces/hzhongresearch/auditoryhum_samples
AHEAD-DS and OpenYAMNet paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10360
AHEAD-DS: https://huggingface.co/datasets/hzhongresearch/ahead_ds
AHEAD-DS (unmixed): https://huggingface.co/datasets/hzhongresearch/ahead_ds_unmixed
OpenYAMNet/YAMNet+ models: https://huggingface.co/hzhongresearch/yamnetp_ahead_ds



Licence
See LICENCE.txt in each subproject.


Contact
For questions, feedback, or collaboration opportunities, please contact: - Name: Romaric Bouveret - Email: romaric.bouveret@mq.edu.au

title: User Guide
text:
This guide assumes you are a researcher or participant in an experiment using PRISM. It is expected a local instance of PRISM is running, as described in the Developer Guide, and you have access to it.
PRISM helps researchers collect real-world data through a smartphone application aimed to gently catch particiopants in a moment of hearing difficulty. PRISM can capture a snapshot of your real-world environment in that moment. This includes:
This data helps build the PRISM machine learning framework to automatically recognise difficult listening situations and adjust hearing aids in real-time.
The PRISM web frontend serves as the interface for data collection and study management for Researchers conducting “experiments” within the PRISM ecosystem. Researchers use this application to design experiments, manage study cohorts, and deploy tasks to Participants. It is the control center for your data collection needs.

title: User Guide
text:
Participant User Guide
Download the PRISM App to your device. Then follow the instructions to participate in a research experiment.

Step 1: Join the experiment
Once the participant clicks the invite link from their email, they will be able to see this interface. Click “Open”.



Join the experiment




Step 2: Starting the experiment



Starting the experiment



The Recording Period: This is the block of time (Like a 4 hour window)
The Recording Session: Those are the individual “sessions” (short recordings) that happen inside it.



Step 3: Sending a recording



Sending a recording




Step 4: Finish the session



Finish the session
